title: Mild to Moderate Hypothermia
immediate_steps: Move the person to a warm, dry environment
Remove wet clothing and replace with dry clothing or blankets
Apply warm, dry compresses to the center of the body (chest, neck, groin)
If fully conscious and able to swallow, give warm, sweet, non-alcoholic beverages
avoid: Do not rub or massage the skin
Do not apply direct heat (like a fire, heating pad, or hot water bottle directly on skin)
Do not warm the arms and legs first (this forces cold blood back to the heart)
Do not give alcohol or caffeine

title: Severe Hypothermia
immediate_steps: Handle the person extremely gently (rough handling can cause cardiac arrest)
Prevent further heat loss by wrapping in blankets
If breathing has stopped, begin CPR immediately
Continue CPR until emergency responders arrive, even if the person feels ice cold and appears dead ('no one is dead until they are warm and dead')
avoid: Do not actively rewarm the person rapidly
Do not give up on CPR

title: Frostbite
symptoms: Skin turns red, then white, pale, or grayish-yellow
immediate_steps: Remove wet clothing and restrictive items (rings)
Gradually warm the area using body heat (e.g., place frostbitten fingers in armpits)
Alternatively, soak the area in warm water (100°F - 105°F) for 15-30 minutes until skin becomes soft and returns to a normal color
Loosely bandage the area with sterile dressings; separate fingers or toes with gauze
avoid: Do not rub the skin or massage it
Do not use dry heat (fire, radiator, heating pad)
Do not walk on frostbitten feet
Do not thaw the area if there is a chance it will refreeze before getting medical help
